purpose: Classifies financial positions by vulnerability to financing conditions, identifying which structures are inherently fragile and prone to discontinuous failure
components: Hedge finance: income covers both principal and interest — survives rate increases
Speculative finance: income covers interest but requires refinancing of principal — vulnerable to credit availability
Ponzi finance: income covers neither principal nor interest — depends entirely on asset appreciation or continued cheap financing
Paradox of Tranquility: stability breeds instability as agents migrate from hedge to speculative to Ponzi positions during calm periods
Applied classification: basis trade at 50-100x leverage is Ponzi finance — income cannot service debt under even modest repo rate volatility
current_assessment: Basis trade cleanly fits Ponzi finance classification. However, the XIV/short-vol counterexample (challenger_02) shows not all Ponzi-classified unwinds produce systemic contagion. Central bank put (revealed preference for accommodation in every stress episode since 2008) may prevent the Minsky transition from completing. Framework is descriptive of current vulnerability but does not predict timing of transition.
limitations: Does not predict timing or triggers for the transition from stability to crisis
The Paradox of Tranquility is descriptive, not predictive — cannot distinguish late-stability from pre-crisis
Central bank put may prevent transitions from completing, which the original framework does not address
Not all Ponzi-classified positions produce systemic crises when they unwind
Single metric (income-to-debt-service) may oversimplify multi-dimensional risk profiles
